methods.description: Process for collecting pollen data from Claytonia virginica flowers across various sites to study pollen removal and deposition dynamics.

steps.description: We collected data in a number of sites in suburban Philadelphia, Maryland, and North Carolina in Spring 2009 and Spring 2010.
steps.searchable_summary: collect pollen data from Claytonia virginica flowers at specified sites

steps.description: Data collection began with the start of anthesis in the C. virginica population and when female-phase flowers in the population were open and ended when female-phase flowers in the population were closing.
steps.searchable_summary: collect data during anthesis and female-phase flower opening

steps.description: The daily activity window began as early as 8:45 AM on warm and sunny days, but on cool days or after rain this often did not occur until late morning or early afternoon.
steps.searchable_summary: adjust data collection timing based on weather conditions

methods.description: Statistical analysis of pollen removal and deposition by different pollinator groups using generalized linear models and mixed models.

steps.description: We compared the number of pollen grains removed and deposited by pollinator groups using generalized linear models (GLMs).
steps.searchable_summary: perform GLM analysis on pollen data by pollinator groups

steps.description: We used a negative binomial error distribution for both the removal and deposition models because the response variable in both data sets was overdispersed.
steps.searchable_summary: apply negative binomial distribution to GLM models

steps.description: For removal and deposition models, we used the R function glm.nb in the library MASS and conducted multiple comparisons using the glht function in the library MULTCOMP.
steps.searchable_summary: use R functions glm.nb and glht for model analysis

steps.description: To evaluate how the hour of collection affected the number of grains remaining in male-phase flowers, we used generalized linear mixed models (GLMMs) with time since anthesis as the predictor.
steps.searchable_summary: perform GLMM analysis on pollen data with time since anthesis as predictor

steps.description: We used the function glmmADMB in the glmmADMB library because it allowed us to include random effects and account for overdispersion using a negative binomial distribution.
steps.searchable_summary: use glmmADMB for mixed model analysis with random effects
